coredata.dc:title: Liquid-liquid extraction and purification of oil red O derived nitrogen-doped highly photoluminescent carbon dots and their application as multi-functional sensing platform for Cu2+ and tetracycline antibiotics
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Lijuan
Zeng, Jing
Quan, Tian
Liu, Shaochi
Deng, Linlin
Kang, Xun
Xia, Zhining
Gao, Die
coredata.dc:description: Oil red O was applied as precursor to synthesize nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) by a facile solvothermal method for the first time. Surprisingly, liquid–liquid extraction and purification resulted in the quantum yield (QY) of NCDs up to 68%. The purification mechanism is based on the difference in polarity and solubility between precursor, over-carbonized substances and NCDs. Further, optical properties and structure characterizations of NCDs were characterized by ultraviolet (UV) spectroscopy, fluorescence (FL) spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and X-ray diffraction (XRD). Importantly, NCDs can be acted as an outstanding multi-functional sensing platform for Cu2+ and tetracycline antibiotics (TCs). Compared with other coexisting metal ions and antibiotics, this platform showed special responses to Cu2+ and TCs. NCDs showed high selectivity and sensitivity of fluorescence response to Cu2+ over a broad range of 10 nM −50 μM and a satisfactory detection limit (LOD)of 4 nM. Besides, NCDs also showed high sensitivity for the detection of TCs with a LOD range of 50 pM-100 nM. In addition, the fluorescent quenching mechanism of Cu2+ and TCs to NCDs were identified as IFE and static quenching. Finally, the platform was successful applied to detect Cu2+ and TCs in actual samples (e.g., water, milk, urine) with satisfactory results. All results indicated that NCDs appeared to have tremendous potential for Cu2+ and TCs detection in various fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Oil red O
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Liquid–liquid extraction
High quantum yield
Cu2+
Tetracycline antibiotics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation, liquid–liquid extraction and purification of NCDs
Determination of Cu2+ and TCs in actual samples
Exploring the NCDs doped agarose hydrogel (NCDs-AH) for the qualitative analysis of Cu2+
Fluorescence response of NCDs to Cu2+
Fluorescent determination of Cu2+ by using NCDs
Possible quenching mechanism for the detection of Cu2+
Fluorescence response of NCDs to TCs
Sensitivity and selectivity for the determination of TCs
Possible fluorescence quenching mechanism for the detection of TCs
Detection of Cu2+ and TCs in real samples
Detection of Cu2+ in actual samples
Detection of TCs in actual samples
Exploring the NCDs doped agarose hydrogel (NCDs-AH) for the detection of Cu2+

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21904109 and No. 21974015), the Luzhou science and technology bureau innovation miao sub-project (NO. 2019-RCM-92) and a joint program of Luzhou Government and Southwest Medical University 2020LZXNYDJ25.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Relationships between the integrated fluorescence intensity and UV absorbance of NCDs.

A. TEM image of NCDs (inset is the diameter distribution of NCDs calculated from TEM image). B. HR-TEM image of NCDs. C. XRD pattern of NCDs. D. The FT-IR spectra of oil red O (curve a), formamide (curve b) and NCDs (curve c). E. XPS spectra of NCDs (inset is the element content table). F. C1s spectrum of the NCDs. G. N1s spectrum of the NCDs. H. O1s spectrum of the NCDs.

A. UV–vis absorption of NCDs. B. Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of NCDs (insets show the NCDs solution under visible light and UV light of 365 nm). C. Fluorescence emission spectra of NCDs with different excitation wavelengths (from 310 nm to 400 nm).

A. Fluorescence emission spectra of NCDs in the presence of Cu2+ with the concentration of 0–50 μM. B. The relationship curve between F0/F and concentration of Cu2+. C. Selectivity of NCDs for Cu2+ in the presence of metal ions (50 μM). D. Overlap between the absorption band of Cu2+ and the excitation or emission band of the NCDs. E. Fluorescence decay curves of NCDs and NCDs with Cu2+.

A. Fluorescence emission spectra of NCDs in the presence of demeclocyline with the concentration of 0–100 μM. B. Fluorescence emission spectra of NCDs in the presence of tetracycline with the concentration of 0–100 μM. C. Fluorescence emission spectra of NCDs in the presence of minocycline with the concentration of 0–100 μM. D. Fluorescence emission spectra of NCDs in the presence of doxycycline with the concentration of 0–100 μM. E. Fluorescence emission spectra of NCDs in the presence of oxytetracycline with the concentration of 0–100 μM. F-J. The relationship curves between F0/F and concentration of demeclocyline (F), tetracycline (G), minocycline (H), doxycycline (I) and oxytetracycline (J).

A. Image of agarose hydrogel under the light. B. Image of agarose hydrogel under UV lamp at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm. C. Image of agarose hydrogelafter treating with Cu2+-water solution (the concentration of Cu2+ is 1 mg·L−1) under UV lamp at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm. D. Image of NCDs doped agarose hydrogel (NCDs-AH) under UV lamp at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm. E. Image of NCDs doped agarose hydrogel (NCDs-AH) after treating with Cu2+-water solution (the concentration of Cu2+ is 1 mg·L−1) under UV lamp at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm.
A. Preparation, liquid–liquid extraction and purification of NCDs. B. Application of NCDs as multi-functional sensing platform for the detection of Cu2+ and TCs.
Recoveries of Cu 2+ in environmental water samples by using the NCDs based sensor (n = 3).
Recoveries of TCs in milk, urine and environment water samples by using the NCDs based sensor (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Liquid-liquid extraction and purification of oil red O derived nitrogen-doped highly photoluminescent carbon dots and their application as multi-functional sensing platform for Cu2+ and tetracycline antibiotics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan 646000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan 646000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Chongqing University, Chongqing 401331, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Chongqing University, Chongqing 401331, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Oil red O was applied as precursor to synthesize nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) by a facile solvothermal method for the first time. Surprisingly, liquid–liquid extraction and purification resulted in the quantum yield (QY) of NCDs up to 68%. The purification mechanism is based on the difference in polarity and solubility between precursor, over-carbonized substances and NCDs. Further, optical properties and structure characterizations of NCDs were characterized by ultraviolet (UV) spectroscopy, fluorescence (FL) spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and X-ray diffraction (XRD). Importantly, NCDs can be acted as an outstanding multi-functional sensing platform for Cu2+ and tetracycline antibiotics (TCs). Compared with other coexisting metal ions and antibiotics, this platform showed special responses to Cu2+ and TCs. NCDs showed high selectivity and sensitivity of fluorescence response to Cu2+ over a broad range of 10 nM −50 μM and a satisfactory detection limit (LOD)of 4 nM. Besides, NCDs also showed high sensitivity for the detection of TCs with a LOD range of 50 pM-100 nM. In addition, the fluorescent quenching mechanism of Cu2+ and TCs to NCDs were identified as IFE and static quenching. Finally, the platform was successful applied to detect Cu2+ and TCs in actual samples (e.g., water, milk, urine) with satisfactory results. All results indicated that NCDs appeared to have tremendous potential for Cu2+ and TCs detection in various fields.

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), a typical fluorescent nanomaterial with outstanding fluorescent properties, have been widely applied in the fields such as sensing, catalysis, bio-imaging [1–3]. However, applications of semiconductor QDs have been limited because the preparation process of them is complex and they are toxic in a way [4]. Carbon dots (CDs), a fascinating class of nano-fluorescent materials, exhibit optical properties equivalent of semiconductor QDs. They have the properties of low toxicity, simple preparation process, low preparation cost, good water solubility, photostability and biocompatibility in comparison with semiconductor QDs [5]. These superior properties make CDs extraordinary be widely used in the application fields of biosensor, bio-imaging and drug delivery [6]. Moreover, CDs also show outstanding performance in the fields of catalysis, optoelectronic devices manufacturing and sensing (e.g. electrochemical sensing) due to the good electrochemical and photochemical properties of CDs [7,8]. Especially, the field of sensor for various substances such as metal ions [9], environmental pollutants [10], antibiotics [11] is a focus research area due to the selectivity and high sensitivity of CDs.

It is worth noting that high quantum yield (QY) is one of the crucial prerequisites for sensor application of CDs because CDs with high QY demonstrate higher sensitivity for target analytes [12]. Hence, various researchers devote themselves into developing CDs with high QY by using various precursors. Especially, heteroatom doping is an effective measure to improve the QY of CDs, and doping of CDs with N, S, B or other heteroatoms can regulate the surface chemical structure and carbon frameworks of CDs, resulting in the improvement of the QYs [13,14]. Therefore, development of CDs with high QYs by using precursors with heteroatoms (single, two and three heteroatoms) have gained much attention. Besides, the preparation and purification processes of CDs can also affect their final QYs. In particular, the purification process of CDs plays an important role in removing impurities from CDs and further improving their QYs. Dialysis and silica column chromatography are two common methods of purification. Although CDs can be purified effectively through dialysis, sometimes the reaction solvent and unreacted raw materials to the preparation of CDs cannot be completely removed. In addition, silica column chromatography is still a cumbersome process for purifying CD. Interestingly, liquid–liquid extraction is an effective way to remove reaction solvent and unreacted raw materials. The difference in polarity and solubility between raw materials, over-carbonized substances and NCDs can result in the separation of CDs from other substances. Moreover, compared with dialysis and silica column chromatography, liquid–liquid extraction need short period of time, the active groups on the surface of CDs are not easy to change (e.g., oxidation). Therefore, liquid–liquid extraction could be an another measure to purify CDs with high QY.

Recently, water pollution which caused by excessive discharge of Cu2+ is becoming increasingly serious [15]. Cu2+ in the water can pass through various channels into the human body, resulting in the accumulation of Cu2+ in human body. Moreover, the excessive presence of Cu2+ can lead to a variety of diseases such as Alzheimer’s disease, Wilson’s disease [16]. Hence, the United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has already stipulated the safe concentration of Cu2+ content in drinking water (20 μM, 1.3 mg·L−1). Till now, various methods (e.g. atomic absorption spectroscopy and ICP-MS) have already developed for Cu2+ detection [17]. However, most of them are limited by multistep sample preparation and require professional technical operation [18]. It is gratifying that attributed to the preponderances of low-cost, low time consumption and cheap instrument, fluorescence detection method which bases on various fluorescent materials stands out [19]. Thus, CDs with high QY need to be developed for the sensitive detection of Cu2+.

The rapid development of aquaculture results in the increased concentrations of antibiotics such as tetracyclines (TCs) [20], sulfonamides [21,22] in water environment, and the sharply growing antibiotic accumulation has caused more and more concern. Especially, due to the merits of low cost and oral administration, TCs have been broadly used for the treatment of infections for humans and animals. Some of TCs also are used as growth promoter for livestock [11,23]. However, because of its widespread use and their non-biodegradable nature, TCs are easily accumulated in foods (e.g. milk), water samples, even residue in the human body [24,25]. The heavy and overuse of TCs may pose many adverse effects such as hepatic damage and promote bacterial resistance, resulting in damage to the environment flora balance [26]. Hence, the residues and accumulations of TCs in food and environment are serious safety problems faced by human. Recently, methods such as HPLC [27] and LC-MS [28] have already developed for TCs detection. These methods are faced with the problems such as the requirement of expensive equipment for some methods, complex sample pretreatment processes and long analysis time, limiting the application in rapid and real-time determination [29]. It is gratifying that CDs based fluorescent sensor shows outstanding advantages such as fast detection speed, high sensitivity, simply detection method and does not require sample pretreatment. Hence, CDs based fluorescent sensor can be demonstrated to be a feasible measure for fast, effective determination of TCs. Till now, various CDs based fluorescent sensors have already been applied in TCs detection [30–33]. However, most of the existing CDs are still not sensitive enough, and little research has been applied to the detection of demeclocycline (DMC) and minocycline (MC). Therefore, new CDs with high QY which have high sensitivity to the detection of various TCs need to be developed.

Oil red O, a kind of fat-soluble azo dye, can effectively dissolve fat and for fat dyeing, specific to dyeing of neutral triglycerides, lipid and lipoprotein stain in tissues and cells. The presence of nitrogen source makes oil red O a good precursor for the preparation of CDs. Herein, highly-efficient blue emissive nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were prepared via a facile solvothermal method by using oil red O as precursor and formamide as solvent. The NCDs were effectively purified through liquid–liquid extraction and purification method. Subsequently, a series of characterization was used to verify whether NCDs were successfully prepared. Due to the prominent fluorescent properties and abundant functional groups, NCDs were applied as the multi-functional sensing platform for Cu2+ and TCs. Fluorescent detection and selectivity experiments were further used to evaluate the effectiveness of the sensing platform. Importantly, in order to study the fluorescent quenching mechanisms of Cu2+ and TCs to NCDs, UV–vis spectra and fluorescence lifetimes were conducted. Finally, experiments of practical application of NCDs based multi-functional sensing platform in different water, milk and urine samples systems were also investigated.

Oil red O (purity > 99.9%), oxytetracycline (OTC, > 98%), tetracycline hydrochloride (TC, >97%), glycine (>99%) and other antibiotics (>98%) were gained from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. Doxycycline hydrochloride (DC, > 98%) was purchased from BBI Life Sciences. Demeclocycline hydrochloride (DMC, >98%) and minocycline hydrochloride (MC, > 98%) were supplied by Aladdin Corporation (Shanghai, China), Quinine sulfate (purity>99%) was bought from Acros Co. Ltd. Reagents related to ions were obtained from Kelong Chemical Co. Ltd. All aqueous solutions were made with deionized water.

Transmission electron microscope with a model of JEOL 2100F (JEOL) was utilized to obtain the TEM images. The functional groups on the surface of NCDs (FT-IR spectra) were acquired by an IR Affinity-1S FT-IR spectrophotometer (Shimadzu). XRD data was observed from a X-ray diffractometer with the mode of Bruker D8 advance (Bruker). The ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra of NCDs were performed on a UV-2600 spectrometer (Shimadzu). The elements of NCDs were identified by using a K-Alpha+ X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo fisher Scientific). A LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer manufactured by PerkinElmer was applied to perform the fluorescence properties of NCDs. A FLS980 lifetime and steady state spectrometer manufactured by Edinburgh Instruments Ltd., was utilized to gain the fluorescence lifetimes of NCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation, liquid–liquid extraction and purification of NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: A simple solvothermal method was used to synthesize NCDs by using oil red O as precursor and formamide as solvent, respectively. Firstly, 2 g of oil red O was added into 30 mL of formamide and then ultrasonic for 30 min to dissolve oil red O. Thereafter, the mixture was placed into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave for reaction at the temperature of 200 °C for 2 h. After that, the dark-brown solution after cooling was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove insoluble precipitate. Then, the solution was filtered through a membrane filter (0.22 μm). Next, the collected solution was applied to liquid–liquid extraction. The liquid–liquid extraction process was as followed: Firstly, five times the volume of dichloromethane was added into the collected solution, and the mixture was transferred into separating funnel. After shaking and let stand for 10 min, the dichloromethane and formamide were separated. The black formamide layer was repeatedly extracted for three times until the colour of dichloromethane layer did not change. The dichloromethane layer was transferred to a round-bottom flask and dried for one hour at 60° C on a rotary evaporation dryer until the liquid volume no longer decreased. The yellow–brown NCDs solution was collected and sealed for later use. The preparation processes and applications of NCDs are shown in scheme 1 .
The QY of NCDs was acquired by using a slope method with quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY = 54%) as the standard fluorophore. The detail can be seen in Supporting materials .

NCDs were dissolved in deionized water in the whole analysis (NCDs concentration was set as 1 mg·L−1). To investigate the sensitivity of NCDs for Cu2+, different concentrations of Cu2+ with a range of 0–50 μM (300 μL) were added into the NCDs solutions (2.7 mL). Fluorescence spectrometer was used to measure the fluorescence intensity of solutions before and after adding Cu2+ at room temperature. In addition, different metal ions (including Fe3+, Fe2+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, K+, Ag+, Ge2+) were selected to investigate the selectivity of NCDs for Cu2+. The F0/F was used to measure the ability of Cu2+ to quench the fluorescence intensity of NCDs. F0 and F refer the fluorescence intensities of NCDs in the absence and presence of Cu2+, respectively. On the other hand, fluorescent “off–on” sensor which based on NCDs-Cu2+ system was designed for the evaluation the presence of Cu2+ in the system with a lot of interferences. In details, 300 μL of amino acid (glycine) at 100 μM was added into the mixture solution of NCDs-Cu2+ solution (2.7 mL, concentration of Cu2+ was set as 50 μM). The fluorescence recovery effect of glycine was recorded. All of the fluorescence spectra were acquired at 360 nm (excitation wavelength).

To explore the fluorescence quenching effects of TCs for NCDs, 300 μL of different concentrations (0–100 μM) of TCs (including DMC, TC, MC, DC and OTC, structures are illustrated in Fig. S1
in
supporting material ) were mixed with NCDs solution (2.7 mL) separately. The fluorescence quenching effects were recorded. To explore the selectivity of NCDs for the detection of TCs, other kinds of antibiotics including amoxicillin, norfloxacin, erythromycin, sulfadiazine, kanamycin, streptomycin, norfloxacin and neomycin were determined at the concentration of 50 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cu2+ and TCs in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: The actual samples of tap water (tap water in laboratory), river water (Derived from Tuojiang River) and local lake water in Zhongshan Park (Luzhou, Sichuan Province, China, where a lot of carp are raised) are used for Cu2+ testing. In addition, the actual samples of milk (purchased from the local supermarket) and urine are used for TCs testing. Before detection, the water actual samples need to be pretreatment. Firstly, the insoluble impurities in water samples were removed through centrifugation at 1000 rpm for 30 min and then filtered using a 0.45 μm filter membrane. Thereafter, the standard addition method was used to evaluate the practical applicability of the sensing platform for Cu2+ and TCs detection. In details, different concentrations of Cu2+ and TCs were separately added into the actual sample for the measurement. The fluorescence intensities were measured at excitation wavelength of 360 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploring the NCDs doped agarose hydrogel (NCDs-AH) for the qualitative analysis of Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: 10 μL of NCDs was added into 10 mL of boiling agarose (2%) -deionized water solution (concentration of NCDs were 1 mg·L−1). Thereafter, 2 mL of NCDs solution and solution without NCDs were added into 6-well plates. After cooling, the NCDs-AH and AH (without of NCDs) were put into 15 mL of water with and without Cu2+ (concentration of Cu2+ was 1 mg·L−1, lowing than the safe concentration of Cu2+ content in drinking water, 1.3 mg·L−1). Finally, the changes of fluorescence responses of NCDs-AH and AH under UV lamp were observed.

It has been fully proved that the introduction of nitrogen can increase the QY of prepared CDs [34], and a surface defect caused by active groups such as-NH2, –COOH can provide more possibilities for NCDs luminescence [35]. Therefore, oil red O (rich in nitrogen), coupled with formamide (a common solvent for NCDs synthesis), was used for the synthesis of NCDs by bottom-up method at 200 °C for 2 h for the first time. Meanwhile, two step liquid–liquid extraction was applied to the purification of NCDs. Significantly, the QY of CDs extracted by two step liquid–liquid extraction increases from 18% (untreated) to 68%, while the QY of NCDs after dialysis is 33% (Shown in Fig. 1 ). The possible reasons are as follows: in the first step liquid–liquid extraction process, because of different polarity, formamide, over-carbonized substances and fluorescent small molecules were in the upper part of the parting funnel. Inversely, the uncarbonized oil red O and NCDs are in the lower part. In the second step of rotary evaporation, as dichloromethane vaporizes, the oil red O comes out of the solution as solid. However, due to the amino and hydroxyl groups on the external NCDs form intermolecular hydrogen bonds, the melting and boiling point of NCDs increase, and the NCDs exist in the form of red-brown liquid eventually. Finally, the higher purity NCDs were obtained. Moreover, according to the Table S1, it can be obviously seen that the QY of the NCDs is higher than the CDs which prepared by using other precursors coupled with formamide. The reasons for the obtain of high QY of NCDs can be concluded as: 1) Doping is an effective measure to improve the QY of CDs as it can introduce the emissive energy traps to facilitate the emissive recombination of localized electron hole pair [34]. 2) Liquid-liquid extraction can more effectively to remove impurities.

TEM and HRTEM were adopted to observe the morphology of NCDs. As illustrates in Fig. 2
A, NCDs is fairly uniform and the particle size distribution ranges from 2 to 7 nm (average diameter is around 4.5 nm) (shown in inset of Fig. 2 A). The HRTEM image (Shown in Fig. 2 B) presents that NCDs have obviously lattice fringes and the lattice spacing is 0.21 nm, a typical distance between graphite (100) planes. The results illustrate that the crystal structure of the NCDs is mainly made up of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms and has a graphite-like structure [36]. XRD analysis was conducted to verify the crystallization property of NCDs. As shown in Fig. 2 C, the XRD pattern of NCDs shows that a sharp diffraction peak exists at 2θ = 27.3°, which is corresponded to the diffraction peak of the (002) diffraction pattern of graphite carbon. The result indicates that NCDs contain sp2 hybrid carbon core and has graphite-like structure.

The surface structures of NCDs were measured by FT-IR spectra. As displays in Fig. 2 D, the bands at 3444 cm−1 to 3300 cm−1 are correspond to O–H and N–H stretching vibrations of NCDs (curve c of Fig. 2 D). The two peaks at 1695 cm−1 and 1586 cm−1 belong to the stretching vibrations of the amide C = O bond in NCDs, demonstrating the existence of amide group (–CO-NH-) on the surface of NCDs (curve c of Fig. 2 D). Meanwhile, the induction effect of C = O leads to the decrement of electron cloud density around N–H, resulting in the shift of the absorption peak of –NH2 on formamide to high frequency (3474 cm−1, shown in curve b of Fig. 2 D) in comparison with NCDs. Thus, formamide may also participate in the synthesis of NCDs. Comparing the FT-IR spectrum of NCDs with that of oil red O (curve b), the absorption bands which are attributed to the bending vibration of C–H of benzene ring at 853 cm−1 and 784 cm−1 almost disappear, demonstrating that oil red O is almost completely carbonized into NCDs. In general, the FT-IR results demonstrate that the surfaces of NCDs are full of various functional groups such as N–H, O–H, –CO–NH– and CO, which not only improve the possibility of luminescence for NCDs, but also increase the hydrophilicity and stability of NCDs.

In addition, XPS spectra were further conducted to determine the composition and chemical state of elements in NCDs. As depictes in Fig. 2 E, the full spectrum shows three sharp peaks which separately correspond to the 1 s electron binding energy of C (285 eV), N (399 eV) and O (531 eV) atoms. The contents of them are 76.11%, 13.78% and 10.11%, respectively. The high-resolution split peak analysis of C1s peak is shown in Fig. 2 F. It can be easily seen from Fig. 2 F that four peaks at 284.3 eV, 285.1 eV, 286.6 eV and 288.0 eV confirm the presence of chemical bonds of C = C/C–C, C-N, C-OH and C = O, respectively. Three peaks centered 398.4 eV, 399.1 eV, and 399.9 eV in N1s spectrum are belonging to pyrrole N, amino N and pyridine N, respectively (Shown in Fig. 2 G) [37]. Further, three peaks of 531.2 eV (C = O), 532.0 eV (C-OH), 532.8 eV (C-O) appear in O1s (Shown in Fig. 2 H) [36]. The above XPS data are clearly coincided with the characterization results of FT-IR

The absorption and fluorescence properties of CDs were explored by analyzing UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. The UV spectrum of NCDs (shown in Fig. 3
A) presents an obvious absorption peak at about 245 nm and a wide absorption peak at 360 nm, which are assigned to the π-π* electronic transition of CC, CN and n-π* electronic transition of CO (COH), respectively [38]. Meanwhile, the fluorescence spectra in Fig. 3 B displays that the maximum emission wavelength of NCDs is at 430 nm with the excitation of 360 nm. Moreover, aqueous solutions of the NCDs are colorless and transparent, while bright blue fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp can be obtained (inset of Fig. 3 B). In addition, when the excitation wavelength changes from 310 nm to 400 nm (shown in the Fig. 3 C), the emission peak wavelength is red-shifted, which is a typical excitation-dependent emission. This phenomenon is due to the chemical composition of the surface and different emission traps [39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of NCDs to Cu2+

ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. S2
A indicates that the fluorescence intensity of NCDs can be quenched by adding Cu2+. To obtain the optimal quenching detection time, different time are applied to conduct the quenching experiment. As indicates in Fig. S2
B, NCDs can be quenched within 1 min and the quenching efficiencies remain unchanged for the detection time of 10 min. The results demonstrate that the response time for NCDs to Cu2+ is fast and 1 min can be used as the detection time. The pH effect on the fluorescence intensity of NCDs was studied to evaluate the stability of NCDs for further detection. As depictes in the Fig. S2
C and Fig. S2
D, due to the inhibition of electron transfer between NCDs functional groups by protonation, the fluorescence intensity decreases nonlinearly with the decrease of pH under 4. Unlike other NCDs fluorescence tests for Cu2+, the fluorescence intensity is unaffected by pH when the pH between 4 and 10, which means that NCDs is stable and is not affected by pH in the detection of actual samples (pH > 4). Furthermore, Fig. S2
E shows that the optimal concentration of CDs for Cu2+ detection is 1 mg⋅L−1, which can be further used in subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent determination of Cu2+ by using NCDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Under the optimal conditions, the sensitivity experiment of the NCDs to Cu2+ (Concentration of Cu2+ ranged from 0 to 50 μM) was conducted. As shown in Fig. 4
A, the fluorescence intensity of NCDs-aqueous solution decreases sharply after adding Cu2+. The relationship between the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F, F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity without Cu2+ and with Cu2+, respectively) of NCDs and the concentrations of Cu2+ is exhibited in Fig. 4 B. Good linearity with linear equation of F0/F = 0.04002 [C] + 0.97143 (R 2 = 0.9902) can be obtained. The LOQ (limit of quantitation) of NCDs is 10 nM and the LOD of Cu2+ was determined as 4 nM based on the formula of 3σ/slope. Ascribed to the high fluorescence QY of NCDs and the easy binding effect of Cu2+ to the carbonyl group on NCDs, NCDs show better sensitivity to Cu2+ in comparison with other CDs (and fluorescent materials) (Shown in Table S2).

Because actual samples usually contain many other metal ions, the anti-interference ability of NCDs on the detection of Cu2+ were investigated before done the actual samples. The fluorescence intensities were measured by separately adding different metal ions with the concentration of 0.5 mM ((including Fe3+, Fe2+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, K+, Ag+, Ge2+) into NCD solutions. It can be seen that from Fig. S3
A and Fig. 4 C that Cu2+ has the most prominent fluorescence quenching effect on NCDs, whereas other metal ions have no distinct fluorescence changes. The results indicate that other metal ions have no influence on the fluorescence detection of Cu2+. Moreover, 0.5 mM Cu2+ was added to a solution of NCDs which contains 0.5 mM concentration of other metal ions to appraise the selectivity of NCDs for Cu2+. The results in Fig. S3
B and Fig. 4 C reveal that other metal ions in actual samples may (Concentration of them in actual samples are much lower than 0.5 mM) have little effects on the quenching effect of Cu2+ to NCDs, indicating the high selectivity and good anti-interference performance of NCDs for Cu2+ detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible quenching mechanism for the detection of Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para:
In general, the fluorescence quenching mechanisms of CDs are mainly divided into inner flter effect (IFE), fluorescence energy resonance transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), static quenching and dynamic quenching. UV–vis spectra can be used initially to determine whether there are IFE, FRET and PET, because they have a feature of certain degree of overlap between the excitation or emission band of the fluorophore and the absorption band of the quencher [40]. Moreover, it is reported that if the quenching mechanism contains FRET, PET and dynamic quenching, the fluorescence lifetimes of CDs with and without quenchers will change. However, the fluorescence lifetimes in IFE and static quenching will not change. Thus, the IFE and static quenching can be distinguished from FRET (PET and dynamic quenching) by evaluation the fluorescence lifetimes of CDs with and without quenchers [41]. As shown in Fig. 4 D, the UV–vis absorption band of Cu2+ has a centrain degree of overlap with the excitation wavelength of NCDs ranges from 300 nm to 410 nm, indicating that the IFE, FRET and PET may exist in the quenching mechanism. However, as shown in Fig. 4 E, the fluorescence lifetime of NCDs decreases from 9.33 ns (without adding Cu2+) to 8.63 ns (with adding Cu2+). Noticeably, little change of fluorescence lifetimes demonstrates that the quenching mechanism may be from IFE and static quenching. To further confirm the static quenching mechanism, the Stern-Volmer equation (Expressed as follows) can be used to evaluate the fluorescence quenching.
F0/F = 1 + Ksv[c] = 1 + Kqτ0[c]
where τ0 is the average lifetime of NCDs. Kq is the quenching rate constant. Ksv refers to the quenching constant. Kq and Ksv are obtained by doing the detection at different temperatures. It is reported that static quenching may happen when Ksv decreases with the increment of temperature [42]. As includes in Fig. 4 F and Table S3, the Ksv (2.64 × 104, 2.23 × 104 and 1.94 × 104 for 20, 30 and 40 °C, respectively) gradually decreases with the increment of temperature from 20 °C to 40 °C. Moreover, the maximum Kq of collision quenching is 1–2 × 1010 L⋅mol−1⋅s−1. Three Kq in present experiment are much larger, further confirming the existence of static quenching [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of NCDs to TCs

ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. S4
A-E show that the fluorescence intensity of NCDs can be quenched by adding TCs. Moreover, NCDs can be quenched within 1 min by TCs and the quenching efficiencies remain unchanged for 10 min (Shown in Fig. S4
F-J). The results demonstrate 1 min can be used as the detection time for TCs detection. Besides, as the fluorescence intensity of NCDs is unaffected by pH when the pH between 4 and 10, so the TCs concentrations in water samples can be effectively measured. In addition, Fig. S5
A-E show that the optimal concentrations of CDs for TCs detection are 1 mg⋅L−1, which is consistent with the concentration of CDs used in the detection of Cu2+. Hence, 1 mg⋅L−1 is further applied as CDs concentration in all TCs detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity for the determination of TCs
ce:section.ce:para:
Under the optimal conditions, the sensitivity of the NCDs responses to TCs was systemic investigated by adding various concentrations of TCs (0–100 μM). Fig. 5
A-E displays that with the concentration of TCs increase, the fluorescence intensities of NCDs decline gradually. The relationships between F0/F (where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity without TCs and with TCs, respectively) and the concentration of TCs are shown Fig. 5 F-J. It is obviously seen that good linearity between F0/F and the concentration of TCs in a certain range are obtained, which satisfies the linear square F0/F = a [C] + b (R2 > 0.990). C is the concentration of TCs, a and b are constants. The LODs of DMC, TC, MC, DC and OTC are 50 pM, 500 pM, 5 nM, 50 nM and 100 nM, respectively. In comparison with previously reported literatures (listed in Table S4), the LODs and detection ranges of TCs here are satisfactory.

Because of the complexity of the actual environment system, the NCDs should not only have high sensitivity to TCs, but also have high selectivity and anti-interference property. To access the selectivity of NCDs to TCs (TC was used as a sample), other kinds of antibiotics (50 μM) including amoxicillin, enrofloxacin, erythromycin, sulfadiazine, kanamycin, streptomycin, norfloxacin and neomycin were added into NCDs solution. As demonstrated in Fig. S6
A and Fig. S6
B, the fluorescence of TC-NCDs is quenched, and these possible interfering compounds have almost no remarkable effect, demonstrating the good selectivity for TCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible fluorescence quenching mechanism for the detection of TCs
ce:section.ce:para:
From the results of FT-IR and XPS, it can be easily seen that NCDs contain hydrophilic groups (- OH, –NH2, –CO-NH-) on the surface. Moreover, TCs (Structure is shown in Fig. S1 ) which are rich in hydroxyl and carbonyl groups can coexist with NCDs in a system and combine with each other through hydrogen bonds. This may be the first step of fluorescence quenching, and hydrogen bond binding may contribute to subsequent quenching mechanism. To further confirm the fluorescence quenching mechanism, UV–vis spectra and fluorescence lifetimes were also conducted. As shown in Fig. S7
A-E, TCs have obvious absorption bands at about 270 nm and 360 nm, these absorptions are obviously overlapped the excitation spectrum of the NCDs. The results indicate that the IFE, FRET and PET may exist in the quenching mechanism. However, the fluorescence lifetime of NCDs remains basically unchanged after adding TCs (Shown in Fig. S7
F-J), demonstrating that IFE and static quenching may be the main mechanisms of fluorescence quenching. In addition, according to the Stern-Volmer equation of TC (Doxycycline is the representative), the static quenching exists in the fluorescence quenching process (Shown in Fig. S8 and Table S5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ and TCs in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Interestingly, due to the strong complexation between Cu2+ and amino acid (Glycine is the representative), glycine can rescue the Cu2+ from NCDs, and form the complex of Cu2+-glycine. Eventually, the weak fluorescence of NCDs can be partially recovered. As shown in the Fig. S9 , the fluorescence can be partially recovered with the addition of glycine to the NCDs-Cu2+ system, Hence, NCDs-Cu2+-glycine system can be used to preliminarily determine whether the actual sample exists Cu2+ or not, further solving the problem of false positives caused by the presence of TCs and Cu2+ in the actual sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: It is important to evaluate the ability of fluorescence sensor in detecting Cu2+ in real water samples because there are many potential disturbances. As there are no Cu2+ in selected water (Tap water, river water, and local lake water) samples, different concentrations of Cu2+ (0.5 mg·L−1, 1 mg·L−1 and 2 mg·L−1) were added to the real water samples to appraise the practicability of NCDs based sensor. The results of actual addition at different concentrations of Cu2+ are presented in Table 1 . It is found that the recoveries of Cu2+ range from 97.50% to 118.32%, and the relative standard deviations range from 3.55% to 6.05%. These results indicate that the sensor has good accuracy and can effectively detect Cu2+ in real environment water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TCs in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: Milk (purchased from the local supermarket) and urine were selected as real samples to evaluate the practicability of NCDs based sensor for TCs detection. It is well known that biological samples are complex, which make the detection of target analytes difficult. Hence, because no TCs were detected in selected samples, different concentrations of TCs (5 μM, 10 μM and 15 μM) were added to the real samples. As shown in Table 2 , the recoveries of TCs are between 89.80% and 111.20%, and the relative standard deviations are less than 6%. The experimental data indicate that NCDs has the ability to detect TCs in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploring the NCDs doped agarose hydrogel (NCDs-AH) for the detection of Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: It is easily seen that above developed methods for Cu2+ and TCs detection can only be used for off-site analysis in the laboratory. However, in many cases, it is necessary to carry out sample analysis on-site, especially for water samples. Therefore, in order to use NCDs for on-site monitoring, NCDs were doped into a hydrogel to form NCDs doped agarose hydrogel (NCDs-AH), pictures are shown in Fig, 6). Here, Cu2+ was used as a detection object to evaluate the test the practicality of NCDs-AH. As shown in Fig. 6 , NCDs-AH shows bright blue fluorescence under UV lamp at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm. However, after treating with water (containing 1 mg·L−1 of Cu2+), the fluorescence of NCDs-AH is obviously quenched. As the safe concentration of Cu2+ content in drinking water is 1.3 mg·L−1, the NCDs-AH can be used for the visualization analysis of Cu2+ in water samples. Moreover, the only equipment needed is a UV lamp, the on-site analysis can be done. These results demonstrate that NCDs-AH based on-site analysis provides a new way for the application of NCDs.

In this study, NCDs with highly blue fluorescence were successfully prepared via a facile one-step solvent thermal method using oil red O as the precursor for the first time. Importantly, the QY of NCDs is as high as 68% after liquid–liquid extraction. The purification principle is based on the different solubility of NCDs from raw materials and over-carbonized substances in formamide and methylene chloride. The surface defect state of NCDs caused by rich –NH2 and –OH on the surface is the main luminescence mechanism of NCDs. Cu2+ can strongly quench the fluorescence of NCDs with a LOD of 4 nM. Moreover, NCDs-AH provides an on-site visualization qualitative analysis of Cu2+ in water with a portable UV lamp. Furthermore, NCDs can also sensitively detect TCs with a wide detection range of 100 pM to 50 μM due to IFE and static quenching. Finally, NCDs were successfully utilized as sensitive fluorescent sensor for the detection of Cu2+ and TCs in actual samples with satisfactory results. In general, NCDs can be applied as a multi-functional sensing platform for the sensitive and prompt detection of Cu2+ and TCs.

Lijuan Yang: Validation, Visualization. Jing Zeng: . Tian Quan: Data curation. Shaochi Liu: Data curation. Linlin Deng: . Xun Kang: . Zhining Xia: Supervision. Die Gao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21904109 and No. 21974015), the Luzhou science and technology bureau innovation miao sub-project (NO. 2019-RCM-92) and a joint program of Luzhou Government and Southwest Medical University 2020LZXNYDJ25.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106391.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The fluorescence mechanism of carbon dots, and methods for tuning their emission color: a review
Microchim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Yan, Z. Sun, H. Zhang, X. Sun, Y. Jiang, Z. Bai, The fluorescence mechanism of carbon dots, and methods for tuning their emission color: a review, Microchim Acta, 186 (2019). 10.1007/s00604-019-3688-y.
